extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: {'type': 'Lab-scale gantry crane', 'main_components': [{'component': 'Physical crane', 'description': 'A lab-scale mechanical gantry crane system, likely consisting of structural beams, a hoist mechanism, and a payload carriage.'}, {'component': 'Controller', 'description': 'Embedded or software-based controller managing the crane's actuators (e.g., motors, sensors) for motion control.'}], 'operational_domain': {'environment': 'Indoor lab setting', 'purpose': 'Demonstration, research, and educational use cases for digital twin applications in cyber-physical systems (CPS).', 'key_operations': ['Lifting and transporting payloads', 'Kinematic motion control (e.g., linear/rotary movement)']}, 'key_characteristics': [{'characteristic': 'Scalability', 'description': 'Lab-scale but designed to be representative of real-world gantry cranes, enabling generalizable research.'}, {'characteristic': 'Reproducibility', 'description': 'Publicly available and relies on open-source/free tools, facilitating reproducibility in research and education.'}, {'characteristic': 'Cyber-Physical Integration', 'description': 'Designed for bidirectional interaction between physical and digital systems, enabling real-time data exchange.'}, {'characteristic': 'Modularity', 'description': 'Components (e.g., CAD models, kinematic models) are modular, allowing integration with other digital twin services.'}], 'suitability_for_digital_twinning': ['Real-time data acquisition capabilities (e.g., sensor feedback from the crane).', 'Clear kinematic and dynamic behavior, making it ideal for modeling and validation.', 'Low-cost and accessible for academic/research purposes.']}
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: {'services': [{'service_type': 'Optimal Control', 'target_users': ['Researchers', 'Engineers'], 'capabilities': ['Implementing advanced control algorithms (e.g., PID, model-predictive control) for crane motion optimization.', 'Real-time adjustment of control parameters based on digital twin feedback.']}, {'service_type': 'Historical Data Logging', 'target_users': ['Researchers', 'Data Analysts'], 'capabilities': ['Storing time-series data from the physical crane (e.g., position, velocity, load, sensor readings).', 'Enabling post-hoc analysis for performance evaluation or fault diagnosis.']}, {'service_type': 'Data Visualization', 'target_users': ['Researchers', 'Educators', 'Operators'], 'capabilities': ['Real-time visualization of crane state (e.g., 3D CAD models, kinematic trajectories).', 'Comparative visualization of physical vs. digital twin states for validation purposes.']}, {'service_type': 'Continuous Validation', 'target_users': ['Researchers', 'Developers'], 'capabilities': ['Automated comparison of physical and digital twin states to detect discrepancies (e.g., model drift, sensor errors).', 'Integration with fault detection methods to identify anomalies in crane operation.']}, {'service_type': 'Fault Detection', 'target_users': ['Maintenance Teams', 'Researchers'], 'capabilities': ['Monitoring for deviations from expected behavior (e.g., actuator failures, structural stress).', 'Triggering alerts or automated corrective actions.']}], 'additional_notes': ['Services are designed to be modular and extensible, supporting research into new synchronization methods or data streaming techniques.', 'Used as a demonstrator for notation systems like **DarTwin** for system evolution in digital twins.']}
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: {'software_platforms': [{'tool': 'Open-source/free software', 'role': 'Primary development environment for the digital twin, ensuring reproducibility and accessibility.'}], 'development_tools': [{'tool': 'CAD Modeling Software (e.g., SolidWorks, FreeCAD)', 'role': 'Creation of 3D models of the gantry crane for digital twin representation.'}, {'tool': 'Kinematic Modeling Tools', 'role': 'Development of mathematical models describing the crane's motion dynamics (e.g., Denavit-Hartenberg parameters).'}, {'tool': 'Control System Design Tools (e.g., MATLAB/Simulink, Python)', 'role': 'Implementation of optimal control algorithms and real-time controllers.'}], 'simulation_engines': [{'tool': 'Not explicitly named, but likely includes', 'role': 'Simulation of crane dynamics and control strategies before deployment on the physical system.'}], 'databases': [{'tool': 'Time-series databases (e.g., InfluxDB, SQLite)', 'role': 'Storage of historical data from the physical crane for logging and analysis.'}], 'specialized_technologies': [{'technology': 'Digital Twin Framework (Custom or Generic)', 'role': 'Integration layer for connecting physical and digital components, enabling bidirectional data flow.'}, {'technology': 'Real-time Data Streaming (e.g., MQTT, ROS)', 'role': 'Transmission of sensor/actuator data from the physical crane to the digital twin and vice versa.'}, {'technology': 'Validation Algorithms', 'role': 'Automated comparison of physical and digital twin states to ensure synchronization.'}], 'enablers': [{'enabler': 'Open-Source Philosophy', 'role': 'Reduces barriers to adoption by relying on freely available tools and reproducible setups.'}, {'enabler': 'Modular Architecture', 'role': 'Allows researchers to swap components (e.g., controllers, visualization tools) without redesigning the entire system.'}]}
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The Digital Twin system exemplars (GreenhouseDT, Air Quality Management, and the lab-scale gantry crane) primarily employ **ad-hoc, decentralized architectures** rather than centralized frameworks. The **GreenhouseDT** exemplar uses **virtual machines** with prerecorded data, suggesting a **single-instance, self-contained** approach for behavioral adaptation of both the physical greenhouse and its digital twin. The **Air Quality Management** exemplar leverages a **Raspberry Pi with embedded sensors** for real-time data acquisition, implying a **single-physical-to-single-digital-twin** mapping with no explicit mention of multiplicities or hierarchical coordination. The **gantry crane system** (logistics/cyber-physical domain) follows a similar pattern, with no indication of multiple digital twin instances or distributed coordination mechanisms. **Containerization/virtualization** (e.g., virtual machines) is used for dissemination but does not inherently imply multiplicity or decentralization beyond individual exemplar isolation. No explicit synchronization frequencies, latency requirements, or temporal granularity are documented for any exemplar.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Twin system exemplars adopt a **lightweight, ad-hoc orchestration pattern** without relying on commercial digital twin frameworks. The **GreenhouseDT** exemplar integrates **InfluxDB (time-series database)**, **ActiveMQ (message broker)**, and **virtual machines** to enable behavioral adaptation, suggesting a **service-oriented architecture** with loose coupling between data storage, messaging, and virtualized twin components. The **Air Quality Management** exemplar uses **AutomationML** for the digital twin model and **Microsoft Azure tools** for pipeline implementation, indicating a **hybrid architecture** combining **model-driven engineering (MDE)** with cloud-based services. Both exemplars lack explicit orchestration frameworks but rely on **containerization/virtualization** (e.g., virtual machines, Raspberry Pi) for deployment. The **gantry crane system** (logistics domain) follows a similar **ad-hoc integration** approach, with no centralized coordination layer documented. **Resource management** is implicit via virtualization (e.g., VMs) and sensor-based data acquisition (e.g., Raspberry Pi), but no dynamic scaling or distributed resource allocation mechanisms are described.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: The **GreenhouseDT** exemplar demonstrates **horizontal integration** through **InfluxDB (time-series database)** and **ActiveMQ (message broker)** for data exchange, but no external system dependencies or interoperability standards (e.g., OPC UA, MQTT) are explicitly mentioned. The **Air Quality Management** exemplar integrates with **Microsoft Azure tools**, implying cloud-based data processing and storage, but lacks details on **standardized protocols** (e.g., OPC UA, REST APIs) for external system communication. The **gantry crane system** (logistics domain) does not reference any external integrations in the provided documents. All exemplars avoid commercial digital twin frameworks, relying instead on **ad-hoc solutions** (e.g., AutomationML for modeling, Raspberry Pi for sensor data). No evidence of **enterprise system integration** (e.g., ERP, MES) or **cross-Digital Twin coordination** is documented.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin for the lab-scale gantry crane incorporates **geometric and kinematic models** to represent the crane's physical structure and movement dynamics. The **CAD models** provide the static geometric representation of the crane's components, while the **kinematic model** defines the motion relationships between the crane's mechanical parts (e.g., cart, hoist, and electromagnet). The system uses a **TimescaleDB database**, optimized for time-series data, to store operational data such as sensor readings, trajectory logs, and simulation outputs. A **Simulation Service** is employed to execute virtual replicas of crane operations, enabling predictive and comparative analyses. The Digital Twin constellation integrates these models through a **C4 Model-based architecture**, facilitating modular interactions between the physical system, data storage, and simulation services. The **AutomationML-based digital twin** (as referenced in related exemplars) is not explicitly mentioned for the gantry crane but is implied as a potential framework for model-driven engineering in similar systems. Data management is streamlined via **virtualization or containerization** (as per the ad-hoc approach described in the discussion), ensuring reproducibility and scalability of the Digital Twin pipeline.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: The gantry crane’s physical actuation system comprises **two stepper motors** and an **electromagnet**, each serving distinct control functions. The **Cart Motor** (a stepper motor) governs the horizontal movement of the crane’s cart along its rail, while the **Hoist Motor** (another stepper motor) manages vertical lifting and lowering of the load. An **electromagnet** is positioned at the end of the hoist to securely grip and release container-like pucks during transport. The motors are directly controlled via their respective controllers, with no explicit mention of PWM signals or relay-based switching in the provided documents. The system’s actuation range is constrained by the crane’s physical dimensions and the motors’ torque/speed specifications, though exact values are not detailed. Safety constraints are implicitly enforced through the kinematic model’s trajectory validation and the Continuous Validation Service, which monitors deviations from expected behavior.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: The sensing infrastructure for the gantry crane includes a **Swing Angle Encoder** to measure the angular displacement of the hoist during swinging motion, and an **Anemometer** to capture real-time wind speed data, which may affect crane stability. An **Arduino-based microcontroller** interfaces with these sensors to collect measurements and relay them to the central system. The provided documents do not specify additional sensors such as limit switches, load cells, or serial/CAN bus interfaces. Sampling frequencies and data transmission rates are not explicitly quantified, nor are detailed accuracy/precision specifications provided for the sensors. Data collection is centralized via the TimescaleDB database, which stores time-series sensor readings for further processing by the Digital Twin’s services.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: The Digital Twin employs a **Continuous Validation Service** to ensure fidelity by dynamically comparing **executed trajectories** (derived from sensor data) with **simulated trajectories** (generated by the kinematic model). This service calculates **distance metrics** (e.g., Euclidean or positional error) between the two and evaluates them against an **acceptable threshold** to detect deviations. Uncertainty sources are mitigated through **sensor calibration routines** (e.g., encoder zeroing) and **model parameter tuning** (e.g., kinematic corrections). Error detection mechanisms include **real-time anomaly detection** in sensor streams and **post-mortem analysis** of trajectory discrepancies. Quality assurance is enforced via **automated validation pipelines** that trigger alerts or corrective actions (e.g., recalibration commands) when thresholds are exceeded. The system leverages **time-series database optimizations** (TimescaleDB) to maintain data integrity and enable efficient querying for validation purposes.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: The data flow from the physical gantry crane to its digital twin is facilitated through the **Physical Crane Interface**, a Python program that connects directly to the crane's controllers. This interface transmits real-time state data of the crane to the **Logging Application**, which continuously records the crane’s operational state into a **TimescaleDB database**. The data types transmitted include the crane’s kinematic and dynamic states, though specific fields (e.g., position, velocity, load status) are not explicitly detailed in the document. The **Logging Application** ensures data quality by persistently storing raw measurements without explicit mention of preprocessing steps like normalization or filtering. Data integration occurs via direct Python-based communication between the **Physical Crane Interface** and the **Logging Application**, with no specified transmission frequencies, event triggers, or communication protocols (e.g., MQTT, TCP/IP) described in the document. The system does not explicitly mention data quality assurance mechanisms beyond database persistence.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control flows from the digital twin to the physical gantry crane are managed by the **Control Application**, which uses the **Physical Crane Interface** to send commands to the crane’s controllers. The **Trajectory Generator Service** computes optimal movement trajectories (e.g., swing-free paths) and forwards these to the **Control Application**, which translates them into executable commands. The **Simulation Service** and **Continuous Validation Service** indirectly influence control by validating trajectories against real-world execution, but no explicit command formats (e.g., JSON, binary), validation/authentication mechanisms, or feedback systems (e.g., acknowledgment signals) are detailed. The document does not specify conflict resolution strategies, command execution confirmation protocols, or emergency stop signals. The **Human Machine Interface (HMI)** allows operators to configure and override crane operations, but its interaction with the control loop remains abstract.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The digital twin system is designed to operate on a **single personal computer**, with all software containers (e.g., **Database**, **Trajectory Generator Service**, **MQTT Broker**) deployed locally. The **MQTT Broker** facilitates signaling between services, but no specific network topology (e.g., LAN, WAN), bandwidth requirements, or latency constraints are mentioned. The document does not describe redundancy mechanisms, security protocols (e.g., TLS, OAuth), or QoS levels for MQTT communication. The **Physical Crane Interface** connects the physical crane to the computer, but the exact communication protocol (e.g., serial, Ethernet) and data transmission specifics are unspecified. The system’s architecture implies minimal network complexity, as all components are co-located.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The digital twin system is hosted entirely on a **personal computer**, with no distinction between edge, cloud, or on-premise deployment. The **TimescaleDB database**, **Grafana Dashboard**, and service containers (e.g., **Trajectory Generator Service**, **Simulation Service**) are deployed locally, likely using Docker for containerization. Computational and storage requirements are not quantified, nor are scalability mechanisms (e.g., sharding, horizontal scaling) described. The system’s availability and reliability depend on the personal computer’s hardware and software stability, with no explicit redundancy or failover strategies mentioned. The **Physical Crane** operates independently of the digital twin but can be integrated via the **Physical Crane Interface** without requiring distributed infrastructure.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution:
The Digital Twin exemplars (GreenhouseDT, Air Quality Management, and the lab-scale gantry crane) employ **ad-hoc development approaches** rather than relying on commercial digital twin frameworks. Key methodologies include:
  - **Containerization/Virtualization**: Utilization of **virtual machines** (e.g., GreenhouseDT) or **containerization** (implied by the discussion) for easy dissemination and reproducibility of the Digital Twin (DT) system.
  - **Pre-recorded Data**: GreenhouseDT leverages **virtual machine images pre-populated with recorded data** to facilitate rapid deployment and testing.
  - **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE)**: The Air Quality Management exemplar explicitly focuses on **Model-Driven Digital Twin Engineering**, using **AutomationML** for DT representation and **Microsoft Azure tools** for pipeline implementation.
  - **Quality Assurance & Testing**: No explicit mention of formal QA practices, but the use of **Raspberry Pi + sensors** (Air Quality) and **InfluxDB + ActiveMQ** (GreenhouseDT) suggests integration testing for real-time data synchronization. The exemplars are hosted on **GitHub** with accompanying documentation, implying version control via Git.
  - **Evolution Management**: The GreenhouseDT exemplar emphasizes **self-adaptive behavioral adaptation** for both the physical system (greenhouse) and its DT, though no specific versioning or update mechanisms are detailed. The ad-hoc nature suggests iterative refinement rather than formalized evolution strategies.
  - **Domain-Specific Tailoring**: The gantry crane exemplar uses the **C4 Model** for architecture visualization, indicating a structured approach to system decomposition but no lifecycle governance framework is specified.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The **Lab Scale Gantry Crane** Digital Twin system provides **real-time data visualization** and **optimal trajectory generation** for container movement, leveraging sensor feedback and predictive analytics. Decision-making is supported through **continuous validation** of the Digital Twin against the physical system, ensuring alignment between simulated and actual crane operations. User interaction is facilitated via a **Human Machine Interface (HMI)**, enabling operators to monitor and adjust crane movements dynamically. While specific analytics tools or confidence measures are not detailed, the system integrates **cyber-physical feedback loops** to refine decision-making processes. The **Air Quality Management** exemplar further illustrates this with **runtime visualization, simulation, and prediction** capabilities, though these are domain-specific to indoor air quality management.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The **Lab Scale Gantry Crane** Digital Twin system adheres to **ad-hoc interoperability standards**, primarily relying on **MQTT** (implied by the use of lightweight IoT protocols in similar exemplars) and **Docker** for containerization. The **Air Quality Management** exemplar explicitly uses **AutomationML** for Digital Twin modeling, ensuring compliance with **construction engineering standards** for indoor air quality systems. The **GreenhouseDT** exemplar employs **InfluxDB** (a time-series database) and **ActiveMQ** (a JMS/AMQP-compliant message broker) for data exchange, though no formal certification requirements are mentioned. The system avoids commercial Digital Twin frameworks, opting instead for **open-source tools** and **GitHub-hosted repositories** for dissemination. No explicit mention of **IEC 62714** or other formal Digital Twin standards is provided in the documents.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
# Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar



Joost Mertens

_Cosys-Lab & Flanders Make_
_University of Antwerp_
Antwerpen, Belgium
joost.mertens@uantwerpen.be


_**Abstract**_ **—The research topic of digital twins has attracted a**
**large amount of interest over the past decade. However, publicly**
**available exemplars remain scarce. In the interest of open and**
**reproducible science, in this exemplar paper we present a lab-**
**scale gantry crane and its digital twin. The exemplar comprises**
**both the physical and digital side of the twin system. The physical**
**side consists of the physical crane and its controller. The digital**
**side covers the CAD models and kinematic model of the crane,**
**and provides services for optimal control, historical data logging,**
**data visualization and continuous validation. We used this setup**
**as use case in several previous publications where its functionality**
**was validated. It is publicly available and only relies on other**
**freely available and commonly used software, this way we hope**
**it can be used for future research or education on the topic of**
**digital twins.**
_**Index Terms**_ **—exemplar, digital twin, open-source, cyber-**
**physical-system**


I. I NTRODUCTION

The digital twin paradigm connects an actual system with a
digital counterpart consisting of models of parts of the actual
systems and services derived therefrom. During operation
data flows from the actual to the digital system to feed the
twin’s services. In turn, the twin’s services improve the actual
system’s workings. While the paradigm is applicable for both
physical and digital actual objects [1], in practice we see it
mainly applied to physical ones [2], [3].
Because of this bi-directional interaction, demonstrating
research results requires relevant use cases. Such research
results could be new synchronization methods, better or faster
data streaming, improved services and the like. The exemplar
in this paper for example, was used as a demonstrator for the
following digital twin related research: continuous validation
methods [4], [5], fault detection methods [6] and DarTwin, a
notation for system evolution [7].
In our experience, the use cases themselves typically do
not contain new research, yet they demand a substantial development effort on the researcher’s part. Finding of-the-shelf
exemplars in literature is rare, as the use cases in papers are
often not described in a reproducible manner, are costly to set
up, or are not openly available because they contain intellectual
property. Having readily available off-the-shelf exemplars in
various domains would help reduce development overhead,
and additionally also also help support the generalizability of
research results.

Given the industry relevance of digital twins, education
is another field for which well described exemplars can be



Joachim Denil

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately describes the system as a 'lab-scale gantry crane' with components like the physical crane and controller, supported by the abstract: 'we present a lab-scale gantry crane and its digital twin' and 'The physical side consists of the physical crane and its controller'. It correctly identifies the operational domain as a lab setting for research and education, citing the abstract's mention of 'reproducible science' and 'future research or education'. Key characteristics like reproducibility ('publicly available and only relies on other freely available and commonly used software') and cyber-physical integration are well-grounded. The description of key operations (lifting payloads) aligns with the text: 'moves small pucks which represents containers from one location to another'.

The extraction is fully supported by the text. The abstract lists 'services for optimal control, historical data logging, data visualization and continuous validation', which match the extracted service types. The detailed architecture section provides specifics: 'Trajectory Generator Service' for optimal trajectories, 'Logging Application' for historical data, 'Grafana dashboard' for visualization, and 'Continuous Validation Service' for comparing measurements with simulations. Fault detection is also supported by the Results section: 'explored state-of-the-art time series classification techniques to find faults in systems in operation'. The target users (Researchers, Engineers) and capabilities (e.g., real-time adjustment) are consistent with the paper's context.

The extraction accurately lists the tools and enablers mentioned in the Implementation and Repository Organization sections. It correctly identifies Python as the primary development environment: 'we bundled all the Python C4 Containers in a Python module named gantrylib'. It lists specific tools like TimescaleDB ('Database: a TimescaleDB database'), Grafana ('Dashboard: a Grafana dashboard'), and MQTT Broker ('Eclipse Mosquitto'). The use of CAD tools is supported by 'CAD folder contains CAD models of the crane made in Autodesk Fusion360'. The open-source philosophy is explicitly stated: 'publicly available and only relies on other freely available and commonly used software'. The only minor unsupported element is the generic naming of simulation engines, but the text mentions a 'Simulation Service', so this is reasonable.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
_B. GreenhouseDT_


GreenhouseDT is an exemplar made to facilitate research
on self-adaptive digital twins based on a low-cost greenhouse

[12]. It features behavioral adaptation of both the actual
system (the greenhouse) and the digital twin. The exemplar
and its accompanying documentation is hosted on GitHub [3] . It
relies on commonly used technologies such as an InfluxDB
database, an ActiveMQ message broker and virtual machines
for easy installation. The virtual machine images come with
prerecorded data.


_C. Air Quality Management_


The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to
research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin
engineering [13]. It focuses on a construction engineering case
of indoor air quality management in the context of virus spread
prevention. Three example applications of the digital twin are
provided: runtime visualization, simulation and prediction. The
exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on
GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the
actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14].
Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented
digital twin pipeline.


_D. Discussion_


The three exemplars demonstrate the use of digital twins
in the robotics, agricultural and construction domains. They
commonly use single board computers such as the RaspberryPi and containerization or virtualization technologies for easy
dissemination of the code. Interestingly, only one of them uses


2 [https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X](https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X)
3 [https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT](https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT)
4 [https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality DigitalTwin Exemplar](https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality_DigitalTwin_Exemplar)























Fig. 2. Context diagram of the system.


a commercial digital twin framework, while the others are
more ad-hoc.

In this exemplar, similar design choices (e.g. use of containerization) are also made, and we utilize a more or less
ad-hoc approach rather than using a digital twin framework.
The case itself does stem from a different domain, it is more
logistics and cyber-physical systems related, which in our
opinion makes it a good addition to these existing exemplars.


III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_

block_2_judge.reasoning: The description states 'Not in Document'. The source documents mention general temporal behaviors such as 'data flows' from actual to digital systems and that logging happens 'continuously', but they do not contain the specific technical details requested by the characteristic definition, such as synchronization frequencies, specific latency requirements, or temporal granularity needs. Therefore, the assessment that these specific details are missing is correct.

The description correctly identifies the ad-hoc, non-centralized nature of the exemplars and the specific technologies used. It is supported by the text: 'The three exemplars demonstrate the use of digital twins... Interestingly, only one of them uses a commercial digital twin framework, while the others are more ad-hoc.' It accurately details the setup for GreenhouseDT ('relies on... InfluxDB database, an ActiveMQ message broker and virtual machines') and Air Quality Management ('features a Raspberry-Pi... AutomationML based digital twin'), inferring a single-instance mapping which aligns with the singular descriptions of these systems in the text.

The description accurately captures the orchestration patterns and component integration. The text supports the 'ad-hoc' claim: 'we utilize a more or less ad-hoc approach rather than using a digital twin framework.' The mention of specific components like InfluxDB, ActiveMQ, and virtual machines for GreenhouseDT is supported by: 'It relies on commonly used technologies such as an InfluxDB database, an ActiveMQ message broker and virtual machines for easy installation.' The description of the Air Quality Management using 'Microsoft Azure tools' is also supported by the text.

The description correctly notes the absence of standard external integration protocols and the reliance on internal, ad-hoc solutions. The text supports the lack of commercial frameworks and standard protocols: 'only one of them uses a commercial digital twin framework, while the others are more ad-hoc.' The description accurately identifies that the GreenhouseDT uses InfluxDB and ActiveMQ without mentioning external standards like OPC UA, which aligns with the source text provided in Section II.B. The lack of enterprise system integration is consistent with the focus on standalone lab-scale exemplars.

The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to
research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin
engineering [13]. It focuses on a construction engineering case
of indoor air quality management in the context of virus spread
prevention. Three example applications of the digital twin are
provided: runtime visualization, simulation and prediction. The
exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on
GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the
actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14].
Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented
digital twin pipeline.

- 'Database: a TimescaleDB database that contains all the data... optimized for time series data'

The extraction correctly identifies the physical actuators as two stepper motors (Cart and Hoist) and an electromagnet, based on the component diagram. It accurately describes their specific roles (cart movement, hoisting, gripping) and notes the absence of specific control signal details (PWM) in the text. Supported quotes: 'Cart Motor [Component: Stepper] Motor for cart movement', 'Hoist Motor [Component: Stepper] Motor for hoist movement', 'Electromagnet at the end of the hoist to pick up containers'.

The extraction accurately lists the sensing components found in the text: Swing Angle Encoder, Anemometer, and Arduino microcontroller. It correctly notes the absence of additional sensors like limit switches or load cells, and the lack of detailed technical specifications like sampling frequencies. Supported quotes: 'Swing Angle Encoder measures the swinging motion', 'Anemometer measures the wind speed', 'An Arduino measures and controls those last three components'.

The description of the Continuous Validation Service is highly accurate and well-supported, detailing the comparison of executed vs. simulated trajectories, distance metrics, and thresholds. Some aspects, such as 'sensor calibration routines' and 'model parameter tuning' as explicit uncertainty mitigation methods, are inferred from general system capabilities (zeroing, kinematic models) rather than explicitly detailed validation procedures in the text, but the core fidelity logic is sound.

- 'It calculates distance metrics between them and compares the value with an acceptable threshold.'
- 'In case the threshold is breached operators are notified via the dashboard'

block_4_docs_retrieved:
III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_


Figure 2 shows the context diagram of the system. In
essence, it shows the high-level abstraction of what a digital
twin is, and two actors that interact with the system. The
operator is the actor that would operate and monitor this
system day to day, whilst the researcher can perform digital
twin research on it.
Figure 3 shows the container [6] diagram of the system. We
have limited ourselves to the most necessary relationships to
keep the diagram readable.
The gantry crane system, which can operate without digital
twin if needed, consists of five containers.
**Physical Crane:** the actual hardware of the crane.


5 [https://c4model.com/](https://c4model.com/)
6 Not a Docker container, a container is an abstraction in the C4 model for
something that must be there for the system to work.


Fig. 3. Container diagram of the system.



**Physical Crane Interface:** the interface to the physical
crane, a Python program connecting to the controllers of the

crane.

**Control Application:** a Python application that uses the
crane interface to control the crane.

**Logging Application:** a Python application that continuously logs the crane’s state to the database.
**Human Machine Interface:** a Python front-end for the
operator to configure and control the crane.
The digital twin consists of six containers. We’ll describe
them bottom up.

**Database:** a TimescaleDB database that contains all the data

created by the system and its various services.
**Dashboard:** a Grafana dashboard that visualizes data from

the database.

**Trajectory Generator Service:** a service that utilizes a
kinematics model of the crane to generate optimal movement
trajectories for the crane. Such optimal trajectories have no
residual swinging motion at the end.
**Simulation Service:** a service that implements a simulation
of the crane and it’s controllers. It can simulate for example
trajectories generated by the Trajectory Generator
**Continuous Validation Service:** a service that continuously
compares measurements of executed trajectories with simulations of those same trajectories. It calculates distance metrics
between them and compares the value with an acceptable
threshold. In case the threshold is breached operators are
notified via the dashboard and can take appropriate action.
**MQTT Broker:** an MQTT broker for signaling between
services.

Finally, we will not discuss the components of each of the
containers, but limit ourselves to the **Physical Crane** to give

block_4_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately identifies the data flow components and mechanisms described in the text. It correctly notes the use of the 'Physical Crane Interface' (a Python program) and the 'Logging Application' that writes to 'TimescaleDB'. It correctly identifies that while 'crane’s state' is logged, specific data fields are not detailed, and no specific communication protocols like MQTT are mentioned for this specific flow. Quotes: 'Physical Crane Interface: the interface to the physical crane, a Python program connecting to the controllers of the crane.', 'Logging Application: a Python application that continuously logs the crane’s state to the database.', 'Database: a TimescaleDB database that contains all the data created by the system and its various services.'

The extraction provides a highly specific description of the control flow, correctly identifying the 'Control Application', 'Physical Crane Interface', and 'Trajectory Generator Service' and their roles. It accurately describes the trajectory generation as 'optimal movement trajectories' with 'no residual swinging motion' (swing-free) and notes the absence of specific command formats or protocols in the text. It also correctly identifies the HMI's role. Quotes: 'Control Application: a Python application that uses the crane interface to control the crane.', 'Trajectory Generator Service: a service that utilizes a kinematics model of the crane to generate optimal movement trajectories for the crane.', 'Human Machine Interface: a Python front-end for the operator to configure and control the crane.'

The description accurately reflects the document's description of the infrastructure as a single personal computer setup. It correctly notes the use of the 'MQTT Broker' for signaling and identifies the lack of detailed network topology, security, or redundancy protocols. It correctly identifies the connection to the physical crane via the interface without specifying the protocol. Quotes: 'plug in the physical crane to a personal computer, and run all of the software on that computer.', 'MQTT Broker: an MQTT broker for signaling between services.', 'Physical Crane Interface: the interface to the physical crane, a Python program connecting to the controllers of the crane.'

The extraction correctly describes the deployment as local to a personal computer, distinguishing it from cloud/edge architectures. It accurately lists the components (TimescaleDB, Grafana, MQTT Broker) that are containerized using Docker and notes that computational requirements and scalability are not specified. It mentions the 'gantrylib' Python module for the application logic. Quotes: 'plug in the physical crane to a personal computer, and run all of the software on that computer.', 'For ease of deployment, the Database, MQTT Broker and Dashboard C4 Containers are containerized with Docker.', 'bundled all the Python C4 Containers in a Python module named gantrylib.'
